message.content:
You are a test-discrimination auditor (pass-4 priority dimension). The hc-assay repo at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay has ~500 tests added across 3 hardening passes. Many are "regression tests" that may NOT actually discriminate their fix.

Your job: find tests that PASS even when the code they supposedly guard is broken/reverted.
- Read tests/ and the src they target. For each suspicious regression test, reason about whether reverting the targeted fix would make it fail. If NOT, it's a finding.
- Focus on: tests asserting only return codes/types when the fix is about side effects (fd close, logging, ordering); tests that mock the very thing under test; tests with vacuous assertions (assert truthy on possibly-empty); concurrency tests that pass without the lock; artifact tests (YAML/docs) asserting presence but not stale-absence.
- Also flag any prior-pass regression test whose target fix, if reverted, leaves the test green.

Return JSON findings: id (T-N), severity (critical/high/medium/low), file, line, title, description, poc (the revert that leaves the test green), suggested_fix. Only concrete, file:line-grounded findings.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

1	"""Docs-vs-code drift guards (pass-2 #136, #150, #151, #152, #153, #154).
2	
3	Normative docs make claims a public reader relies on. These guards pin the corrected wording so
4	a regression (re-introducing an over-claim or a stale status) fails CI. They assert on the
5	*specific* stale strings the pass-2 audit found, plus presence of the honest replacement.
6	"""
7	
8	from __future__ import annotations
9	
10	from pathlib import Path
11	
12	_ROOT = Path(__file__).resolve().parent.parent
13	
14	
15	def _read(rel: str) -> str:
16	    return (_ROOT / rel).read_text(encoding="utf-8")
17	
18	
19	def test_data_sovereignty_is_qualified_for_high_stakes_tier():
20	    # #136 + #F-038: the absolute "nothing leaves the machine" over-claim must be qualified — the
21	    # optional high-stakes tier sends prompt content off-box. Pin the SPECIFIC high-stakes caveat
22	    # phrase ("not metered, but off-box"), not a loose "off-box" substring: GOVERNANCE has a
23	    # second, unrelated "off-box" occurrence (the SaaS-observability disqualification), so the loose
24	    # check passed even if the high-stakes-tier caveat itself were deleted.
25	    for rel in ("README.md", "docs/CHARTER.md", "docs/GOVERNANCE.md"):
26	        text = _read(rel)
27	        assert "nothing leaves the machine" not in text, f"{rel}: unconditional over-claim (#136)"
28	        # each file must tie the off-box caveat to the metered/subscription distinction
29	        assert "off-box" in text and "metered" in text, (
30	            f"{rel}: high-stakes off-box / metered caveat missing (#136)"
31	        )
32	    # GOVERNANCE has a SECOND, unrelated "off-box" (the SaaS-observability disqualification), so a
33	    # loose substring passed even if the high-stakes-tier caveat were deleted. Pin its exact
34	    # localized phrase so deleting it fails (#F-038).
35	    gov = _read("docs/GOVERNANCE.md")
36	    assert "not metered, but off-box" in gov, (
37	        "GOVERNANCE high-stakes off-box caveat removed (#F-038)"
38	    )
39	    adr = _read("docs/decisions/ADR-0003-local-first-data-sovereign.md")
40	    assert "off-box" in adr and "the same as" in adr  # billing vs residency separated
41	
42	
43	def test_charter_status_is_not_stale():
44	    # #150: the status line must not still call the seams "stubs being wired".
45	    charter = _read("docs/CHARTER.md")
46	    assert "stubs being wired" not in charter
47	    assert "implemented and composed" in charter or "in place and hardened" in charter
48	
49	
50	def test_baseline_toolkit_scope_is_honest():
51	    # #151 + #F-038: the engine ships primitives + a determinism harness, NOT embedding/clustering/
52	    # graph builders — those are adapter-supplied. Two-sided: assert the POSITIVE attribution
53	    # (the choice-bearing builders come from the adapter's BaselineBuilder, not the engine), so a
54	    # regression re-introducing an "engine ships builders" claim — which leaves "BaselineBuilder"
55	    # in the text and so passed the old single-substring check — now fails.
56	    arch = _read("docs/ARCHITECTURE.md")
57	    init = _read("src/assay_engine/__init__.py")
58	    for text in (arch, init):
59	        assert "BaselineBuilder" in text
60	    assert "not shipped by the engine" in arch, "ARCHITECTURE missing the explicit non-ship caveat"
61	    assert "supplied per study by" in init, "__init__ missing the adapter-attribution caveat"
62	
63	
64	def test_preregistration_timestamp_wording_matches_shipped_default():
65	    # #152: body text must not present RFC-3161 as the in-force mechanism; HMAC is the default.
66	    for rel in ("docs/METHODOLOGY.md", "docs/GOVERNANCE.md"):
67	        text = _read(rel)
68	        assert "HMAC" in text, f"{rel}: shipped HMAC default not stated (#152)"
69	        assert "pluggable" in text, f"{rel}: RFC-3161 must be described as pluggable (#152)"
70	    # Guard the SPECIFIC stale over-claims, so re-introducing either one fails CI even though the
71	    # word "HMAC" appears elsewhere in the file (file-level substring checks alone don't catch a
72	    # localized regression — the pass-2 confirmation flagged this on GOVERNANCE §2 step 3).
73	    gov = _read("docs/GOVERNANCE.md")
74	    assert "RFC-3161 timestamped** against a trusted timestamp authority" not in gov, (
75	        "GOVERNANCE §2 step 3 stale RFC-3161 over-claim reintroduced (#152)"
76	    )

703	def test_close_all_pools_snapshots_under_lock(fake_pg):
704	    # #F-020: _close_all_pools must iterate a SNAPSHOT taken under _init_lock. Discriminating: each
705	    # pool's close() REMOVES itself from _OPEN_POOLS (the same kind of mutation the real failure-
706	    # cleanup path performs). Iterating the live list while removing the current element makes a
707	    # plain `for p in _OPEN_POOLS` SKIP elements, so not every pool gets closed; iterating a
708	    # snapshot closes all of them. We assert every pool was closed exactly once — this FAILS if the
709	    # snapshot is removed (reverted to direct iteration), so the test genuinely guards the fix.
710	    cp, _ = fake_pg
711	    closed = []
712	
713	    class SelfRemovingPool:
714	        def close(self):
715	            closed.append(id(self))
716	            cp._OPEN_POOLS.remove(self)  # mutate the list mid-cleanup, like the real cleanup path
717	
718	    pools = [SelfRemovingPool() for _ in range(4)]
719	    cp._OPEN_POOLS[:] = list(pools)
720	    cp._close_all_pools()
721	    assert sorted(closed) == sorted(id(p) for p in pools)  # ALL closed, none skipped
722	    assert cp._OPEN_POOLS == []  # every pool removed itself

737	def test_submit_bounded_releases_slot_if_callback_registration_is_interrupted(monkeypatch):
738	    # #F-032: if add_done_callback raises (e.g. a KeyboardInterrupt landing in that window), the
739	    # in-flight slot must be released, not leaked — a leak would eventually brick the pool with
740	    # spurious RateLimitErrors.
741	    from assay_engine.reasoning import seam
742	
743	    monkeypatch.setattr(seam, "_inflight", 0)
744	
745	    class _Fut:
746	        def __init__(self):
747	            self.cb = None
748	
749	        def add_done_callback(self, cb):
750	            # mimic CPython: the callback IS appended (will fire on completion) and THEN the
751	            # interrupt lands before returning — so both the explicit fallback _release and the
752	            # later callback target this same future (the double-release window).
753	            self.cb = cb
754	            raise KeyboardInterrupt
755	
756	    fut = _Fut()
757	    monkeypatch.setattr(seam._pool, "submit", lambda fn: fut)
758	    with pytest.raises(KeyboardInterrupt):
759	        seam._submit_bounded(lambda: None)
760	    assert seam._inflight == 0  # slot released despite the interrupted callback registration
761	    # #F-032 follow-up: the appended callback firing later must NOT double-decrement (over-release)
762	    # — _release is per-future idempotent. Simulate the future completing and the callback running.
763	    fut.cb(fut)
764	    assert seam._inflight == 0  # still 0, not -1

50	    # reorder/delete

61	    t.record("verdict", "H1: SUPPORTED", label="SUPPORTED")
62	    recs = list(t.to_records())
63	    # a forger edits the payload and recomputes the chain — but WITHOUT the secret, using the
64	    # unkeyed path — the keyed verify rejects it.

67	    # recompute as an UNKEYED chain (forger has no secret):
68	    rebuilt = ProvenanceTrail(clock=_fixed_clock())

70	    forged_recs = rebuilt.to_records()

72	        forged(forged_recs, secret=secret)  # does not verify under the real key
73	    # the legitimate keyed trail verifies with the secret, and NOT without it
74	    assert from_records(recs, secret=secret)
75	    with pytest.raises(ProvenanceError):

170	def test_record_is_thread_safe_under_concurrency():
171	    # #114: concurrent record() must not corrupt the seq/prev_hash chain.
172	    # A naive concurrency test is non-discriminating under the GIL — the read->append window is
173	    # too narrow to interleave. We inject a clock that sleeps INSIDE that window (record() reads
174	    # seq/prev_hash, then calls the clock, then appends), so an UNLOCKED record() would let
175	    # threads read the same seq and corrupt the chain. With the lock (held across the whole
176	    # _record_locked, clock included) threads serialize and the chain stays intact. Verified
177	    # discriminating: removing the lock makes this fail (duplicate seqs / broken chain).
178	    import threading
179	    import time

181	    def widening_clock():
182	        time.sleep(0.0005)  # widen the read->append window to force interleaving if unlocked
183	        return _dt.datetime.now(tz=_UTC)

186	    n_threads, per = 8, 15

188	
189	    def worker(w):
190	        barrier.wait()  # release all threads at once → maximal contention in the window
191	        for i in range(per):
192	            t.record("k", f"w{w}-{i}", w=w, i=i)
193	
194	    threads = [threading.Thread(target=worker, args=(w,)) for w in range(n_threads)]
195	    for th in threads:
196	        th.start()
197	    for th in threads:
198	        th.join()
199	    assert len(t) == n_threads * per
200	    t.verify()  # intact hash chain despite concurrent appends through a widened window
201	    assert [e.seq for e in t.entries] == list(range(n_threads * per))  # contiguous, no dupes/gaps

196	def _release(future: Any) -> None:
197	    # Idempotent per future (#F-032 follow-up): decrement _inflight AT MOST ONCE for a given
198	    # future. The done-callback path and the interrupt-window fallback in _submit_bounded can BOTH
199	    # reach _release for the same future when add_done_callback appended the callback and then was
200	    # interrupted before returning — without this guard that double-decrements and over-releases a
201	    # slot (the inverse of the leak being fixed). A per-future flag set under the lock makes the
202	    # "_release runs at most once" claim true rather than aspirational.
203	    global _inflight
204	    with _inflight_lock:
205	        if getattr(future, "_assay_released", False):
206	            return
207	        try:
208	            future._assay_released = True
209	        except (AttributeError, TypeError):  # pragma: no cover - Future allows attrs; defensive
210	            pass
211	        _inflight -= 1
212	
213	
214	def _submit_bounded(fn: Callable[[], Any]) -> Any:
215	    """Submit to the shared pool, refusing if every worker slot is already in flight
216	    (hung backend calls would otherwise leak all slots)."""
217	    global _inflight
218	    with _inflight_lock:
219	        if _inflight >= _POOL_WORKERS:
220	            # Saturation is a TRANSIENT condition — surface a retryable backpressure signal so
221	            # _run_with_retries waits and re-tries rather than failing the caller permanently
222	            # (#104). Concurrent callers thus queue briefly instead of erroring out.
223	            raise RateLimitError(
224	                f"reasoning timeout pool saturated ({_inflight}/{_POOL_WORKERS} in-flight) — "
225	                "backpressure; retry shortly"
226	            )
227	        _inflight += 1
228	    try:
229	        future = _pool.submit(fn)
230	    except RuntimeError as exc:  # pool shut down at interpreter exit — normalize, don't leak (#116)
231	        with _inflight_lock:
232	            _inflight -= 1
233	        raise PermanentReasoningError(f"reasoning pool is shut down: {exc}") from exc
234	    except BaseException:
235	        with _inflight_lock:
236	            _inflight -= 1
237	        raise
238	    # Register the slot-release callback under its own guard (#F-032): if a BaseException
239	    # (KeyboardInterrupt/SystemExit) fires while attaching the callback, release the slot
240	    # explicitly so a permanent leak can't accumulate and brick the pool with spurious
241	    # RateLimitErrors. If the interrupt landed AFTER add_done_callback already appended the
242	    # callback (so it will still fire on completion), the explicit _release here and the later
243	    # callback both target this future — _release is per-future idempotent, so the slot is
244	    # released exactly once either way.
245	    try:
246	        future.add_done_callback(_release)
247	    except BaseException:
248	        _release(future)
249	        raise
250	    return future
251	
252	
253	def _with_timeout(fn: Callable[[], Any], timeout: float, what: str) -> Any:
254	    """Run a sync callable with a hard wall-clock timeout via the bounded pool."""
255	    future = _submit_bounded(fn)

2	
3	These guard configuration that lives in .github/workflows/ci.yml and the committed lockfile.
4	The CI YAML cannot be unit-imported, so the guards are twofold:
5	  1. config-level: assert the hardened invocation is present in ci.yml (a revert to the buggy
6	     form fails the test);
7	  2. behavioral: run the exact allowlist-parsing bash loop against fixtures to prove it is
8	     newline-agnostic and comment/blank tolerant (the original `while read -r id` form drops a
9	     final line that lacks a trailing newline).

12	from __future__ import annotations

14	import shutil

24	# The hardened allowlist loop, lifted verbatim from ci.yml's audit job. Kept here so the
25	# behavioral test exercises the SAME logic the workflow runs.
26	_PARSE_LOOP = r"""
27	set -euo pipefail
28	IGNORES=()
29	if [ -f "$1" ]; then
30	  while IFS= read -r id || [ -n "$id" ]; do
31	    case "$id" in ''|\#*) continue ;; esac
32	    IGNORES+=(--ignore-vuln "$id")
33	  done < "$1"
34	fi
35	printf '%s\n' "${IGNORES[@]:-<none>}"
36	"""

39	_PARSE_LOOP_BUGGY = r"""
40	IGNORES=""
41	if [ -f "$1" ]; then
42	  while read -r id; do [ -n "$id" ] && IGNORES="$IGNORES --ignore-vuln $id"; done < "$1"
43	fi

39	    # Discriminating: intercept the module's open() and capture the file handle; after main()
40	    # returns, assert it is .closed. A bare json.load(open(...)) leaves the handle open (closed
41	    # only later by GC), so this fails when the context-manager fix is reverted.
42	    import builtins

269	    for _ in range(200):  # allow done-callbacks to release slots
270	        if rc._inflight == 0:

192	    # #140: a dict-like that is NOT a collections.abc.Mapping (exposes keys()+__getitem__ only)
193	    # must still be scanned by the fence AND deep-frozen — otherwise an Interpretation hidden in
194	    # it slips into a Measurement and the "frozen" value stays a live mutable object.
195	    from assay_engine._frozen import FrozenDict, freeze
196	
197	    class DuckMap:
198	        def __init__(self, d):
199	            self._d = dict(d)
200	
201	        def keys(self):
202	            return self._d.keys()
203	
204	        def __getitem__(self, k):
205	            return self._d[k]

207	    interp = Interpretation(value="judged", basis=(), rationale="r", judged_by="op")
208	    with pytest.raises(TypeError):
209	        Measurement(value=DuckMap({"k": interp}), produced_by="p", inputs_hash="h")
210	    # freeze() must convert a duck-mapping to a FrozenDict, not pass it through as a live leaf
211	    frozen = freeze(DuckMap({"a": 1}))
212	    assert isinstance(frozen, FrozenDict) and frozen["a"] == 1

221	        except Exception as exc:  # noqa: BLE001
222	            failures.append((mod.name, repr(exc)))
223	    assert not failures, f"engine submodules failed to import standalone: {failures}"

1	"""Pre-registration integrity (METHODOLOGY §3, GOVERNANCE §2, ADR-0009).
2	
3	These tests ARE the guarantee: the lock must bind the hypothesis content, attest a verifiable
4	time, and precede confirmation — a hand-set proof string, a post-lock content swap, a forged
5	proof, or a lock dated after the confirmation moment must all be refused.
6	"""
7	
8	import datetime as _dt
9	from dataclasses import replace
10	
11	import pytest
12	
13	from assay_engine.methodology.confirm import confirm_whole_corpus
14	from assay_engine.methodology.hypothesis import Hypothesis, HypothesisKind, HypothesisOrigin
15	from assay_engine.methodology.preregistration import (
16	    LocalHmacAuthority,
17	    PreRegistrationError,
18	    canonical_hypothesis_digest,
19	    lock_hypothesis,
20	    require_preregistered,
21	    verify_preregistration,
22	)
23	
24	_UTC = _dt.timezone.utc
25	
26	
27	def _auth(secret=b"preregistration-unit-test-key-01"):
28	    return LocalHmacAuthority(secret)
29	
30	
31	def _unlocked(hid="H1", **over):
32	    base = dict(
33	        hypothesis_id=hid,
34	        statement="pattern",
35	        kind=HypothesisKind.UNIT_LEVEL,
36	        origin=HypothesisOrigin.DISCOVERY,
37	        test_name="t",
38	        decision_rule="r",
39	    )
40	    base.update(over)
41	    return Hypothesis(**base)
42	
43	
44	def _past():
45	    return _dt.datetime.now(tz=_UTC) - _dt.timedelta(hours=1)
46	
47	
48	# ---- the content digest ----
49	
50	
51	def test_digest_covers_content_and_id_but_not_lock_fields():
52	    h = _unlocked()
53	    # hypothesis_id IS bound (#80): it is the verdict's attribution key, so a swap must be caught
54	    assert canonical_hypothesis_digest(h) != canonical_hypothesis_digest(
55	        replace(h, hypothesis_id="X")
56	    )
57	    # decision-bearing content changes the digest
58	    assert canonical_hypothesis_digest(h) != canonical_hypothesis_digest(
59	        replace(h, decision_rule="r2")
60	    )
61	    assert canonical_hypothesis_digest(h) != canonical_hypothesis_digest(replace(h, statement="s2"))
62	    assert canonical_hypothesis_digest(h) != canonical_hypothesis_digest(
63	        replace(h, predicted_direction="greater")
64	    )
65	
66	
67	def test_digest_is_stable_and_does_not_change_when_locked():
68	    # locking adds locked_at/timestamp_proof, which are EXCLUDED from the digest — so the digest
69	    # the runner recomputes on the locked hypothesis equals the one the proof was issued over.
70	    h = _unlocked()
71	    d_before = canonical_hypothesis_digest(h)
72	    locked = lock_hypothesis(h, authority=_auth(), instant=_past())
73	    assert canonical_hypothesis_digest(locked) == d_before
74	
75	
76	# ---- lock_hypothesis + verify round-trip ----
77	
78	
79	def test_lock_then_verify_roundtrip():
80	    auth = _auth()
81	    locked = lock_hypothesis(_unlocked(), authority=auth, instant=_past())
82	    assert locked.locked
83	    vt = verify_preregistration(locked, authority=auth)
84	    assert vt.authority == "local-hmac"
85	    assert vt.digest == canonical_hypothesis_digest(locked)
86	    assert vt.instant.tzinfo is not None
87	
88	
89	def test_relocking_is_forbidden():
90	    auth = _auth()
91	    locked = lock_hypothesis(_unlocked(), authority=auth, instant=_past())
92	    with pytest.raises(PreRegistrationError, match="already locked"):
93	        lock_hypothesis(locked, authority=auth, instant=_past())
94	
95	
96	# ---- the breaks pre-registration must catch ----
97	
98	
99	def test_unlocked_hypothesis_is_rejected():
100	    with pytest.raises(PreRegistrationError, match="not locked"):
101	        verify_preregistration(_unlocked(), authority=_auth())
102	
103	
104	def test_hand_set_sentinel_proof_is_rejected():
105	    # the exact footgun this module closes: a presence-only "lock" string must NOT verify
106	    fake = _unlocked(locked_at="2026-06-16T00:00:00+00:00", timestamp_proof="rfc3161:demo")
107	    assert fake.locked  # passes the cheap presence predicate ...
108	    with pytest.raises(PreRegistrationError):  # ... but fails real verification
109	        verify_preregistration(fake, authority=_auth())
110	
111	
112	def test_content_swapped_after_locking_is_rejected():
113	    auth = _auth()
114	    locked = lock_hypothesis(_unlocked(), authority=auth, instant=_past())
115	    # keep the proof but change the decision rule — the proof no longer binds the content
116	    tampered = replace(locked, decision_rule="a different, post-hoc decision rule")
117	    with pytest.raises(PreRegistrationError, match="does not bind|does not cover"):
118	        verify_preregistration(tampered, authority=auth)
119	
120	
121	def test_hypothesis_id_swapped_after_locking_is_rejected():
122	    # #80: the id is bound, so a post-lock id swap (which would misattribute the verdict) fails
123	    auth = _auth()
124	    locked = lock_hypothesis(_unlocked(hid="H_original"), authority=auth, instant=_past())
125	    swapped = replace(locked, hypothesis_id="H_DIFFERENT")
126	    with pytest.raises(PreRegistrationError):
127	        verify_preregistration(swapped, authority=auth)
128	
129	
130	def test_non_ascii_mac_field_raises_preregistration_error_not_typeerror():
131	    # #81: a non-ASCII MAC must be a controlled PreRegistrationError, not a leaked TypeError
132	    auth = _auth()
133	    bad = _unlocked(
134	        locked_at="2026-06-16T00:00:00+00:00",
135	        timestamp_proof="local-hmac:v1:2026-06-16T00:00:00+00:00|\udcffmac",
136	    )
137	    with pytest.raises(PreRegistrationError):
138	        verify_preregistration(bad, authority=auth)
139	
140	
141	def test_non_finite_param_raises_preregistration_error():
142	    # #82: a non-finite float in params must surface as a typed firewall error, not a ValueError
143	    auth = _auth()
144	    h = _unlocked(params={"alpha": float("nan")})
145	    with pytest.raises(PreRegistrationError):
146	        canonical_hypothesis_digest(h)
147	    with pytest.raises(PreRegistrationError):
148	        lock_hypothesis(h, authority=auth, instant=_past())
149	
150	
151	def test_proof_from_another_authority_is_rejected():
152	    locked = lock_hypothesis(
153	        _unlocked(), authority=_auth(b"secret-aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa1"), instant=_past()
154	    )
155	    other = _auth(b"secret-bbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbb2")  # different key
156	    with pytest.raises(PreRegistrationError):
157	        verify_preregistration(locked, authority=other)
158	
159	
160	def test_tampered_locked_at_disagrees_with_attested_instant():
161	    auth = _auth()
162	    locked = lock_hypothesis(_unlocked(), authority=auth, instant=_past())
163	    # change the displayed lock time but keep the (now-inconsistent) proof
164	    spoofed = replace(locked, locked_at="2000-01-01T00:00:00+00:00")
165	    with pytest.raises(PreRegistrationError):
166	        verify_preregistration(spoofed, authority=auth)
167	
168	
169	def test_malformed_proof_is_rejected_not_crashed():
170	    for bad in [
171	        "",
172	        "garbage",
173	        "local-hmac:v1:",
174	        "local-hmac:v1:not-a-time|abcd",
175	        "local-hmac:v1:2026-06-16T00:00:00+00:00",
176	        "wrong-prefix:2026|ab",
177	    ]:
178	        with pytest.raises(PreRegistrationError):
179	            verify_preregistration(
180	                _unlocked(timestamp_proof=bad, locked_at="2026-06-16T00:00:00+00:00"),
181	                authority=_auth(),
182	            )
183	
184	
185	# ---- lock-before-confirm ordering ----
186	
187	
188	def test_require_preregistered_accepts_lock_before_confirmation():
189	    auth = _auth()
190	    locked = lock_hypothesis(_unlocked(), authority=auth, instant=_past())
191	    vt = require_preregistered(locked, authority=auth, not_after=_dt.datetime.now(tz=_UTC))
192	    assert vt.instant < _dt.datetime.now(tz=_UTC)
193	
194	
195	def test_require_preregistered_rejects_lock_after_confirmation():
196	    auth = _auth()
197	    future = _dt.datetime.now(tz=_UTC) + _dt.timedelta(hours=1)
198	    locked = lock_hypothesis(_unlocked(), authority=auth, instant=future)
199	    with pytest.raises(PreRegistrationError, match="precede confirmation|strictly before"):
200	        require_preregistered(locked, authority=auth, not_after=_dt.datetime.now(tz=_UTC))
201	
202	
203	def test_require_preregistered_rejects_lock_equal_to_confirmation():
204	    auth = _auth()
205	    when = _dt.datetime.now(tz=_UTC) - _dt.timedelta(hours=1)
206	    locked = lock_hypothesis(_unlocked(), authority=auth, instant=when)
207	    with pytest.raises(PreRegistrationError):  # not strictly before
208	        require_preregistered(locked, authority=auth, not_after=when)
209	
210	
211	# ---- authority hygiene ----
212	
213	
214	def test_naive_instants_are_rejected():
215	    auth = _auth()
216	    with pytest.raises(PreRegistrationError, match="timezone-aware"):
217	        auth.stamp("deadbeef", instant=_dt.datetime(2026, 6, 16, 0, 0, 0))  # naive
218	    locked = lock_hypothesis(_unlocked(), authority=auth, instant=_past())
219	    with pytest.raises(PreRegistrationError, match="timezone-aware"):
220	        require_preregistered(locked, authority=auth, not_after=_dt.datetime(2026, 6, 16))  # naive
221	
222	
223	def test_weak_secret_is_refused():
224	    with pytest.raises(ValueError, match="16 bytes"):
225	        LocalHmacAuthority(b"too-short")
226	
227	
228	def test_confirm_primitive_optional_authority_rejects_fake_lock():
229	    # #84: a direct confirm caller can opt into real verification by passing authority=
230	    auth = _auth()
231	    fake = Hypothesis(
232	        hypothesis_id="H1",
233	        statement="s",
234	        kind=HypothesisKind.WHOLE_CORPUS,
235	        origin=HypothesisOrigin.DISCOVERY,
236	        test_name="t",
237	        decision_rule="r",
238	        predicted_direction="greater",
239	        locked_at="2026-06-16T00:00:00+00:00",
240	        timestamp_proof="rfc3161:demo",  # sentinel
241	    )
242	    null = [0.05 + 0.001 * i for i in range(50)]
243	    # with authority: the fake (presence-only) lock is refused
244	    with pytest.raises(PreRegistrationError):
245	        confirm_whole_corpus(fake, observed=1.0, null_distribution=null, alpha=0.05, authority=auth)
246	    # a genuinely locked hypothesis with the same content passes the gate (verdict is produced)
247	    real = lock_hypothesis(
248	        replace(fake, locked_at=None, timestamp_proof=None), authority=auth, instant=_past()
249	    )
250	    v = confirm_whole_corpus(real, observed=1.0, null_distribution=null, alpha=0.05, authority=auth)
251	    assert v.hypothesis_id == "H1"
252	
253	
254	def test_non_utc_lock_instant_is_normalized():
255	    auth = _auth()
256	    plus5 = _dt.timezone(_dt.timedelta(hours=5))
257	    when = _dt.datetime.now(tz=plus5) - _dt.timedelta(hours=1)
258	    locked = lock_hypothesis(_unlocked(), authority=auth, instant=when)
259	    vt = verify_preregistration(locked, authority=auth)
260	    assert vt.instant.utcoffset() == _dt.timedelta(0)  # normalized to UTC
261	    assert vt.instant == when  # same instant, different representation

1	"""Baseline toolkit — determinism harness + numeric primitives (pure, offline)."""
2	
3	import math
4	
5	import pytest
6	
7	from assay_engine.baseline.determinism import (
8	    build_baseline_artifact,
9	    corpus_fingerprint,
10	    hash_value,
11	    stable_seed,
12	)
13	from assay_engine.baseline.primitives import (
14	    cosine_similarity,
15	    cosine_similarity_matrix,
16	    descriptive_stats,
17	    euclidean_distance,
18	)
19	from assay_engine.contracts.schema import Corpus, Relation, Unit
20	
21	
22	# ---- determinism harness ----
23	
24	
25	def _corpus() -> Corpus:
26	    return Corpus(
27	        units=(Unit("a", "hello", {"k": 1}), Unit("b", "world")),
28	        relations=(Relation("a", "b", "rel"),),
29	        metadata={"v": 1},
30	    )
31	
32	
33	def test_corpus_fingerprint_is_order_independent_and_stable():
34	    c1 = Corpus(units=(Unit("a"), Unit("b")))
35	    c2 = Corpus(units=(Unit("b"), Unit("a")))  # different unit order
36	    assert corpus_fingerprint(c1) == corpus_fingerprint(c2)
37	    # content change -> different fingerprint
38	    assert corpus_fingerprint(c1) != corpus_fingerprint(Corpus(units=(Unit("a"), Unit("c"))))
39	
40	
41	def test_corpus_fingerprint_is_byte_stable_across_the_streaming_change():
42	    # #F-016: the fingerprint was changed to stream JSON into SHA-256 (iterencode) instead of
43	    # materializing one big string. This PINS the exact hash for a fixed corpus so the streaming
44	    # change — or any future refactor — that alters the fingerprint bytes fails loudly (every
45	    # existing baseline/provenance record depends on this value being unchanged).
46	    from assay_engine.contracts.schema import Relation
47	
48	    c = Corpus(
49	        units=(Unit("u1", "alpha", attributes={"n": 1}), Unit("u2", "beta")),
50	        relations=(Relation("u1", "u2", "rel"),),
51	        metadata={"src": "fixed"},
52	    )
53	    assert (
54	        corpus_fingerprint(c) == "d4a4b7123ba6248e56e6287aa35774d81db8996de9f1ce630e2fa3bff75f9566"
55	    )
56	
57	
58	def test_stable_seed_is_deterministic_and_distinct():
59	    assert stable_seed("x", "y") == stable_seed("x", "y")
60	    assert stable_seed("x", "y") != stable_seed("x", "z")
61	    assert 0 <= stable_seed("a") < (1 << 32)
62	
63	
64	def test_hash_value_canonical_and_order_independent():
65	    assert hash_value({"a": 1, "b": 2}) == hash_value({"b": 2, "a": 1})
66	    assert hash_value([1, 2]) != hash_value([2, 1])  # sequence order is significant
67	
68	
69	def test_hash_value_is_type_faithful():
70	    # issue #B1: distinct keys sharing a str() must NOT collide
71	    assert hash_value({1: "a", "1": "b"}) != hash_value({1: "b", "1": "a"})
72	    # issue #B2: distinct values sharing a str() must NOT collide (no default=str coercion)
73	    import datetime
74	
75	    assert hash_value(datetime.date(2020, 1, 1)) != hash_value("2020-01-01")
76	    assert hash_value(b"x") != hash_value("b'x'")
77	    # value type matters: int 1 vs str "1" vs float 1.0 vs bool True
78	    seen = {hash_value(1), hash_value("1"), hash_value(1.0), hash_value(True)}
79	    assert len(seen) == 4
80

These pipeline tests are very thorough. Now let me focus on verifying a couple more suspect findings. Let me re-examine candidates:

1. **`test_cosine_matrix_scale_uses_fast_path`** — confirmed finding (perf-only assertion, pure path is 0.08s < 2.0s).

2. **`test_pool_atexit_shutdown_registered_for_reasoning_pool`** (persistence:725) — asserts source string `atexit.register(_pool.shutdown`. This is a source-grep test. If the actual atexit.register line is reverted/removed, the test correctly fails. It's weak but does discriminate the specific line. Lower priority but not a false-pass.

3. **`test_install_flush_on_exit_wires_atexit_and_sigterm`** (observability:162) — the SIGTERM portion is guarded by `if "h" in captured`. If the SIGTERM handler wiring is removed, `captured` stays empty and the SIGTERM assertions are skipped — the test still passes on the atexit portion alone. Let me verify the source to see if SIGTERM wiring being reverted leaves the test green.

Confirmed finding #2. The test at observability:162 mocks `tr.signal.signal` to capture into `captured["h"]`, then runs SIGTERM assertions only `if "h" in captured`. The SIGTERM wiring (source lines 131-142) is the #O3 fix. The test runs in the main thread (pytest), so `captured["h"]` will be set when the fix is present. But if a developer reverts the SIGTERM handler block (lines 131-142) entirely — keeping only the atexit registration — then `signal.signal` is never called, `captured` stays empty, the `if "h" in captured` branch is skipped, and the test passes green. The atexit part alone still satisfies the rest. So the SIGTERM half of this regression test does not discriminate its fix.

Let me verify `captured["h"]` is actually populated in the current (fixed) code path, to be sure the test is currently meaningful but would silently pass on revert. The test only mocks `getsignal` to return SIG_DFL and `signal` to capture. With the fix present, line 141 calls `signal.signal(SIGTERM, _handler)` → captured["h"] set. Confirmed.

Now let me check one more class: the `test_install_flush_on_exit_wires_atexit_and_sigterm` also has `assert calls["flush"] >= 1` after calling the atexit handler — that's fine. The finding is specifically the conditional SIGTERM assertion.

findings.title: test_cosine_matrix_scale_uses_fast_path passes even if the numpy fast path is reverted to the pure O(n^2*d) path
findings.description: The test claims to guard against "a regression to the pure O(n^2*d) path at corpus scale". Its only fast-path-discriminating assertion is `assert elapsed < 2.0`. The remaining assertions (`len(m) == 300`, `m[0][0] == pytest.approx(1.0)`) are satisfied identically by either path. I measured the pure-Python path directly: `_cosine_matrix_pure` on a 300x64 matrix completes in ~0.084s — ~24x under the 2.0s threshold. Therefore reverting the dispatch in `cosine_similarity_matrix` (src/assay_engine/baseline/primitives.py:155) so it always calls `_cosine_matrix_pure` leaves this test GREEN. The test does not discriminate the fix it names; the 2.0s budget is far too loose to detect the pure path, and a real regression on a larger corpus (where the pure path's quadratic cost actually bites) would not be caught here either.
findings.poc: In src/assay_engine/baseline/primitives.py, change `cosine_similarity_matrix` (lines 153-155) to unconditionally `return _cosine_matrix_pure(rows)` (i.e. delete the numpy fast-path branch). `test_cosine_matrix_scale_uses_fast_path` still passes: 300x64 pure-path runtime ~0.08s < 2.0s, and m[0][0] is still 1.0 with dims 300x300.
findings.suggested_fix: Make the test assert the fast path is actually taken rather than timing it. E.g. monkeypatch `assay_engine.baseline.primitives._cosine_matrix_pure` to raise/record and assert it is NOT called when numpy is installed, OR spy on `_cosine_matrix_numpy` and assert it WAS called. Drop the brittle `elapsed < 2.0` wall-clock assertion (it is both non-discriminating and flaky on loaded CI).

findings.title: test_install_flush_on_exit_wires_atexit_and_sigterm passes even if the SIGTERM handler wiring (#O3) is fully reverted
findings.description: The test name and intent assert that BOTH atexit and the SIGTERM handler are wired (the #O3 fix: atexit does not run on `kill`/container-stop, so a main-thread SIGTERM handler is also installed to flush spans). But the SIGTERM half is asserted only inside `if "h" in captured:` (lines 188-190). `captured["h"]` is populated solely as a side effect of the source calling `signal.signal(SIGTERM, _handler)` (src/assay_engine/observability/tracing.py:141). If a developer reverts the entire SIGTERM block (tracing.py:131-142) and keeps only `atexit.register(...)` (line 130), then `signal.signal` is never invoked, `captured` stays empty, the `if "h" in captured` branch is skipped, and the test passes on the atexit assertions alone. So the SIGTERM portion of this regression guard is vacuous on the exact revert it is supposed to catch — the conditional turns a should-be-required assertion into a no-op when the fix is missing.
findings.poc: In src/assay_engine/observability/tracing.py, delete the `if threading.current_thread() is threading.main_thread(): ... signal.signal(signal.SIGTERM, _handler)` block (lines 131-142), keeping `atexit.register(lambda: _safe(flush))`. `test_install_flush_on_exit_wires_atexit_and_sigterm` still passes: atexit handler registered and flush>=1; `captured` is empty so the `if "h" in captured` SIGTERM assertions are skipped.
findings.suggested_fix: Unconditionally require the SIGTERM handler in the main-thread case: since pytest runs the test body on the main thread, assert `"h" in captured` (handler installed) and then exercise it — `captured["h"](signal.SIGTERM, None); assert calls["shutdown"] >= 1`. Remove the `if "h" in captured:` guard so a reverted handler fails the test.

I audited the ~30 test files in /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/tests against their target source. The suite is largely well-constructed — most pass-1..3 regression tests have been deliberately hardened to discriminate (widened race windows, per-thread conn-str resolvers, fd-tracking, two-sided docs guards, behavioral license-gate checks, revert-discrimination on the bash allowlist loop). I found two tests that pass even when the fix they name is reverted.

Findings (both medium):

- T-1 — /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/tests/test_baseline.py:237 `test_cosine_matrix_scale_uses_fast_path`. The only fast-path-specific assertion is `elapsed < 2.0`; I measured the pure-Python path at ~0.084s for the 300x64 input, so reverting `cosine_similarity_matrix` (src/assay_engine/baseline/primitives.py:155) to always use `_cosine_matrix_pure` leaves the test green. Fix: spy that the numpy path is actually taken instead of timing it.

- T-2 — /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/tests/test_observability.py:162 `test_install_flush_on_exit_wires_atexit_and_sigterm`. The SIGTERM half of the #O3 fix is asserted only inside `if "h" in captured:`. Deleting the SIGTERM handler block (src/assay_engine/observability/tracing.py:131-142) means `signal.signal` is never called, `captured` stays empty, the branch is skipped, and the test passes on the atexit assertions alone. Fix: unconditionally require `"h" in captured` (pytest runs on the main thread) and exercise the handler.
